slides.title: Team Protocols
slides.subtitle: Shared Communication Rules
slides.motto: One request-response pattern drives all team negotiation

slides.title: The Problem: Team Protocols
slides.problem:
In s09, teammates work and communicate but lack structured coordination:

**Shutdown**: Killing a thread leaves files half-written and config.json stale. You need a handshake: the lead requests, the teammate approves (finish and exit) or rejects (keep working).

**Plan approval**: When the lead says "refactor the auth module," the teammate starts immediately. For high-risk changes, the lead should review the plan first.

Both share the same structure: one side sends a request with a unique ID, the other responds referencing that ID.

slides.title: The Solution: request_id correlation for two protocols
slides.solution:
```
Shutdown Protocol            Plan Approval Protocol
==================           ======================

Lead             Teammate    Teammate           Lead
  |                 |           |                 |
  |--shutdown_req-->|           |--plan_req------>|
  | {req_id:"abc"}  |           | {req_id:"xyz"}  |
  |                 |           |                 |
  |<--shutdown_resp-|           |<--plan_resp-----|
  | {req_id:"abc",  |           | {req_id:"xyz",  |
  |  approve:true}  |           |  approve:true}  |

Shared FSM:
  [pending] --approve--> [approved]
  [pending] --reject---> [rejected]

Trackers:
  shutdown_requests = {req_id: {target, status}}
  plan_requests     = {req_id: {from, plan, status}}
```
principles: One request-response pattern drives all team negotiation

slides.title: Key Takeaways
takeaways: One request-response pattern drives all team negotiation
Core addition: request_id correlation for two protocols

preview: Teammates scan the board and claim tasks themselves; no need for the lead to assign each one
